question:
$\mathrm{NO}_2$ concentrations (in parts per billion) in the exhaust fumes of different types of vehicle (cars, minibuses and buses) were sampled and the following data were obtained:

\begin{tabular}{lll}
\hline Car & Minibus & Bus \\
\hline 69 & 51 & 41 \\
62 & 45 & 48 \\
73 & 59 & 42
\end{tabular}
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Car & Minibus & Bus \\
\hline 70 & 57 & 39 \\
\hline 68 & 58 & 47 \\
\hline 68 & 54 & 49 \\
\hline 64 & 46 & 35 \\
\hline 71 & 48 & 41 \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(i) Check that the variances of the three samples are not significantly different using an $F_{\text {max }}$ test.
(ii) Calculate a one-way ANOVA on the above data and fill out an ANOVA table:

\begin{tabular}{llllll}
\hline & df & Sums of squares & Mean squares & F value & $P$ \\
\hline Vehicle type & & & & \\
Within & & & & \\
Total & & & & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
(iii) Calculate the mean $(\bar{x})$ and standard error $(S E)$ of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ concentration in each vehicle type:
(iv) Using multiple comparison tests where appropriate, what do you conclude from your analysis of these data?

\begin{tabular}{llllll}
\hline Cars & Minibuses & \multicolumn{3}{l}{ Buses } \\
\hline $\bar{x}:$ & SE: & $\bar{x}:$ & SE: & $\bar{x}:$ & SE: \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
answer:
(i) The highest variance is for minibuses (30.786).

The lowest variance is for cars (12.982).
$F_{\max }=2.37, k=3, \mathrm{df}=7, P>0.05$; therefore the variances are not significantly different.

 egin{tabular}{llllll}
\hline (ii) & $d f$ & Sums of squares & Mean squares & $F$ & $P$ \
\hline Vehicle type & 2 & 2629.750 & 1314.875 & 58.516 & $<0.0001$ \
Within & 21 & 471.875 & 22.470 & & \
Total & 23 & 3101.625 & & & \
\hline
\end{tabular}
(iii)

 egin{tabular}{llllll}
\hline Cars & & Minibuses & & Buses & \
\hline$\overline{ oldsymbol{x}}: 68.1 \mathrm{ppb}$ & SE: 1.27 ppb & $\overline{ oldsymbol{x}}: 52.3 \mathrm{ppb}$ & SE: 1.96 ppb & $\overline{ oldsymbol{x}}: 42.8 \mathrm{ppb}$ & SE: 1.72 ppb \
\hline
\end{tabular}
(iv) There is a significant difference between the $\mathrm{NO}_2$ concentration in the different types of vehicle, with cars having the most and buses the least. Using Tukey's multiple comparisons:
The MSD is smaller than the difference between any pair of means, therefore all the means are significantly different from each other (Cars $>$ Minibuses $>$ Buses; $P<0.05$ )
